sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Plan: Check what other objects did during the animation
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern more clearly:
- Frame 0: White diamond at [4, 17], Orange square at [10, 23]
- Frame 1: White diamond at [57, 27], Orange square at [27, 27]
- Frame 3: White diamond at [57, 27], White square (W, 20 pixels) at [27, 27] - the orange square turned white!
- Frame 5: Orange square back at [27, 27]
- Frame 21: Orange square at [27, 27]
- Frame 22: Everything back to original positions
So when the white diamond moved to [57, 27], the orange square moved to [27, 27] and briefly turned white (frame 3), then went back to orange. The move was ultimately rejected and everything returned to the original state.
This suggests that the white diamond can only move to certain valid positions. The position [57, 27] was not a valid destination. Let me think about what the valid moves might be.
Looking at the current board:
- White diamond at [4, 17]
- Orange square at [10, 23]
- Purple diamond at [39, 48]
- Gray diamonds at [7, 49], [19, 8], [33, 45], [46, 54]
The goal might be to move the white diamond to connect with or transform the gray diamonds. Let me look at the adjacency relationships more carefully.
Actually, I notice that in frame 1, the orange square moved to [27, 27] which is the midpoint between the white diamond's start [4, 17] and the clicked position [57, 27]. Wait, that doesn't quite work. Let me recalculate.
[4, 17] to [57, 27]: the midpoint would be around [30, 22], not [27, 27].
Let me think about this differently. The orange square at [10, 23] moved to [27, 27]. The white diamond moved from [4, 17] to [57, 27]. 
Looking at the gray diamonds: [7, 49], [19, 8], [33, 45], [46, 54]. The clicked position [57, 27] is not near any gray diamond.
Wait, let me re-examine. The initial state had the gray diamond at [57, 27]. But now there are 4 gray diamonds. Maybe the level has multiple stages and I need to complete them in order.
Let me look at the earlier successful transitions to understand the pattern. In transition 0, clicking [21, 39] moved the white diamond to [19, 39]. In transition 1, clicking [40, 33] moved it to [38, 33]. In transition 2, clicking [49, 30] moved it to [47, 30].
These clicks were all near the gray diamond at [57, 27]. The white diamond was approaching it. In transition 3 and 4, the white diamond got very close to [57, 27].
Maybe the goal is to move the white diamond to exactly overlap with a gray diamond. But my click on [59, 27] (near [57, 27]) caused the white diamond to go to [57, 27] in the animation but then return.
Wait - looking at the animation again, the white diamond DID reach [57, 27] but then everything reset. This could mean:
1. The move was invalid because [57, 27] is not a valid target in the current board state
2. The level requires a different approach
Let me look at what the valid targets might be. The gray diamonds are at [7, 49], [19, 8], [33, 45], [46, 54]. Maybe I need to click on one of these positions.
Actually, I think I need to understand the game mechanic better. Let me look at the adjacency list to see which objects are connected.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- White diamond moved to [57,27] in animation but returned - move rejected
- Orange square at [10,23] also moved during animation
- Need to find valid target positions
- Gray diamonds at [7,49], [19,8], [33,45], [46,54]
- Plan: Try clicking on a gray diamond position to see if that's valid

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check adjacency and try clicking on a gray diamond
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
adj = current_frame.segmentation['adjacency_list']

# Build id->info map
id_info = {n['id']: {'color': n['color'], 'pixels': n['pixels'], 'boundary': n['boundary']} for n in nodes}

# Show adjacencies for white diamond and nearby objects
print("Adjacency list (non-background):")
for i, j in adj:
    ci, cj = id_info[i]['color'], id_info[j]['color']
    pi, pj = id_info[i]['pixels'], id_info[j]['pixels']
    if pi > 3 or pj > 3:
        print(f"  {i}({ci},{pi}) - {j}({cj},{pj})")

# Try clicking on the gray diamond at [19, 8] which is closest to white diamond at [4, 17]
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 21, 'col': 8}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
